title: Fragile Self-Esteem
abstract: Abstract We develop a model of fragile self-esteem—self-esteem that is vulnerable to objectively unjustified swings—and study its implications for choices that depend on, or are aimed at enhancing or protecting, one’s self-view. In our framework, a person’s self-esteem is determined by sampling his memories of ego-relevant outcomes in a fashion that in turn depends on how he feels about himself, potentially creating multiple fragile “self-esteem personal equilibria.” Self-esteem is especially likely to be fragile, as well as unrealistic in either the positive or the negative direction, if being successful is important to the agent. A person with a low self-view might exert less effort when success is more important. An individual with a high self-view, in contrast, might distort his choices to prevent a collapse in self-esteem, with the distortion being greater if his true ability is lower. We discuss the implications of our results for mental well-being, education, job search, workaholism, and aggression.
summary_en: Some people have "fragile self-esteem" that swings on small cues — successes make them feel briefly great, failures lead to crashes. This paper models how fragile self-esteem arises. A person samples memories of ego-relevant outcomes, and how they sample depends on how they feel about themselves, creating multiple self-esteem equilibria. Self-esteem is especially fragile when success is important. The paper shows how fragile self-esteem distorts behaviour: low-esteem people exert less effort when success matters more; high-esteem people may distort choices to protect their self-view. A new framework for motivation, procrastination, and self-defeating behaviour.
policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian educational policy and labour-market interventions for disadvantaged youth. The paper's implication: interventions for students or job-seekers with low self-confidence need to build resilient self-esteem, not just deliver information or opportunities. Relevant to KRTK research on Roma youth, to early-childhood education policy, and to adult re-training programs. Caveat: theoretical; specific programme design would need to be grounded in empirical measurement of which Hungarian populations have fragile self-esteem.
